story.headline: Georgia survives Miami by 3 in thriller
body: Georgia found just enough in a 32-29 thriller over Miami in Week 4 PCFL action, with Cohen Lamb earning Player of the Game honors after going for 2 catches for 79 yards and 1 TD.
The Bulldogs were outgained by Miami 503–292 in total offense, including 74 yards on the ground. Miami kept fighting, managing 503 total yards on the night.
Up next, Georgia travels to face LSU in Week 5.

aiStory.headline: Georgia Survives Miami Aerial Assault Behind Lamb's Clutch Catches
body: Miami dominated possession and yardage on Saturday, amassing 503 total yards and holding the ball for stretches that would have buried most opponents. But Georgia's defense bent without breaking, and that discipline proved the difference in a 32-29 decision that hinged on execution in tight moments rather than volume.

The decisive moment came late when Cohen Lamb made two contested catches in the final drive — one for 51 yards along the sideline that moved Georgia into field goal range, then another 28-yard grab that sealed the outcome. Lamb finished with just two receptions but for 79 yards; each one was a rope thrown into traffic that required not just hands but presence of mind. Meanwhile, Miami's Mertz was precise all evening, completing 31 of 46 passes for 343 yards and two touchdowns, but those two Lamb conversions came when Georgia needed to answer, not when Miami was setting the tone.

Georgia's offense was pedestrian — 218 passing yards, 74 rushing — yet the staff kept drives alive through Giles' two touchdown passes and relied entirely on splash plays to move the needle. That gamble works only when your receivers show up when it matters. Miami's Dixon powered through for 98 rushing yards and a score, Jackson added 63 receiving yards, and the ground game stayed dangerous. But one fumble and a failure to punch through late sealed Miami's fate. Sometimes controlling the game isn't enough.
aiStory.turningPoint: Lamb's 51-yard reception down the sideline with under two minutes remaining shifted field position decisively and allowed Georgia to set up the final field goal that won it.

unsungHero.name: Schaefer
unsungHero.why: Nine tackles and relentless gap integrity kept Miami's ground attack from dominating despite 160 rushing yards; his linebacker work in the trenches went unnoticed while Lamb got the highlight reel.

story.headline: Arkansas pulls away from Tennessee, 38-28
body: Arkansas asserted control when it mattered in a 38-28 win over Tennessee in Week 4 PCFL action, with K.J. Jackson earning Player of the Game honors after going for 21 of 36 for 313 yards and 4 TDs.
The Razorbacks outgained Tennessee 505–394 in total offense, including 194 yards on the ground. Tennessee kept fighting, managing 394 total yards on the night.
Up next, Arkansas hosts Texas in Week 5.

aiStory.headline: Arkansas Ground Game Overwhelms Tennessee in Turnover Battle
body: Arkansas dominated the line of scrimmage on Saturday, grinding out 194 rushing yards while forcing three turnovers to secure a 38-28 victory over Tennessee. K.J. Jackson orchestrated the win with surgical precision, completing 21 of 36 passes for 313 yards and four touchdowns without a turnover. The Razorbacks controlled tempo and field position through much of the contest, leaning on their run game to set up play-action opportunities that Jackson exploited downfield.

Tennessee's offense moved the ball effectively in stretches, with MacIntyre throwing for 365 yards and two scores, and Matthews hauling in 109 receiving yards on just four catches. But the Volunteers' inability to protect the football proved fatal. Three fumbles—one of which resulted in a defensive score or short-field situation—shifted momentum at critical junctures and forced Tennessee into reactive football for the second half. Baker managed only 31 yards on six carries, a sign that Arkansas's front seven controlled the line early and often.

The turnover margin was the clearest dividing line. Arkansas's ball security allowed the coaching room to stay ahead of schedule and dictate game flow, while Tennessee's three giveaways against zero by Arkansas meant the Razorbacks consistently operated in favorable field position. MacIntyre's arm talent kept the game competitive, but talent alone could not overcome the operational advantage Arkansas built through discipline and execution.
aiStory.turningPoint: Tennessee's second fumble in the third quarter, which Arkansas converted into a Jackson touchdown pass to Clay, swung momentum decisively and gave Arkansas a two-score lead that the Volunteers could not bridge.

unsungHero.name: Russell
unsungHero.why: While Jackson claimed the spotlight with four touchdown passes, Russell's eight carries for 66 yards—many of them grinding out tough short-yardage conversions—established the physical tone that allowed Arkansas to dominate time of possession and control the game's rhythm.

story.headline: Texas steamrolls Boston College, 43-6
body: Texas dominated from start to finish in a 43-6 rout over Boston College in Week 4 PCFL action, with Jordan Coleman earning Player of the Game honors after going for 1 tackles.
The Longhorns outgained Boston College 425–173 in total offense, including 209 yards on the ground. Boston College never got the offense going, managing 173 total yards on the night.
Up next, Texas travels to face Arkansas in Week 5.

aiStory.headline: Texas Dominates Boston College in Lopsided Week 4 Statement
body: Texas dismantled Boston College 43-6 on Saturday, a performance that revealed the vast gap between these two programs at this stage of the season. The Longhorns controlled every phase, particularly on the ground where they accumulated 209 rushing yards and dictated tempo from start to finish. Boston College's defense simply could not generate consistent pressure, allowing Texas to execute its run-heavy scheme without meaningful disruption.

The Eagles' offensive line struggled to create space for their own ground game, managing just 53 rushing yards on 12 carries. Quarterback Leavitt was forced into a difficult afternoon, completing just 11 of 26 passes for 155 yards as Boston College never established rhythm. The absence of explosive plays—no touchdown passes, no rushing touchdowns—told the story of an offense that lacked both efficiency and creativity against a Texas secondary that moved confidently through coverage.

Texas quarterback Manning orchestrated the offense with precision, hitting 15 of 22 attempts for 216 yards and two touchdowns. Running back Gibson punched in two scores while adding 64 rushing yards, serving as the fulcrum of an attack that never faced serious resistance. The decisive difference came in how each offense moved the ball: Texas consistently converted short-yardage situations and extended drives, while Boston College stalled repeatedly and settled into a defensive crouch that made their evening even longer.
aiStory.turningPoint: Texas's second-quarter touchdown drive, capped by Gibson's one-yard scoring run, pushed the margin to 17-0 and effectively eliminated any realistic comeback pathway for Boston College.

unsungHero.name: Simmons
unsungHero.why: Two sacks in a dominant defensive effort often gets overlooked when the offense scores 43 points, but Simmons's pressure created the conditions for Texas's offensive rhythm by forcing Boston College into predictable third-and-longs.

story.headline: Clemson pulls away from Colorado, 45-28
body: Clemson asserted control when it mattered in a 45-28 win over Colorado in Week 4 PCFL action, with Roger Anderson earning Player of the Game honors after going for 30 of 50 for 312 yards and 4 TDs.
The Tigers outgained Colorado 533–376 in total offense, including 135 yards on the ground. Colorado kept fighting, managing 376 total yards on the night.
Up next, Clemson hosts Oregon in Week 5.

aiStory.headline: Clemson's balance overwhelms Colorado in high-scoring Week 4 affair
body: Clemson's offensive efficiency proved too much for Colorado to match on Saturday, as the Tigers cruised to a 45-28 victory behind a balanced attack that kept the Buffaloes' defense constantly off balance. While Roger Anderson's four touchdown passes for Colorado earned him Player of the Game honors, Clemson's ability to move the ball on the ground and through the air created enough separation to make the final margin comfortable despite Colorado's offensive fireworks.

The telling difference came down to productivity per play. Clemson converted 533 total yards into 45 points with an offense that didn't force the issue, while Colorado's 312 passing yards generated 28 points and required Anderson to shoulder a heavier load with 50 pass attempts. Cortez managed just 18 completions for Clemson but made them count with two touchdowns, and running back Adams Jr. added a ground element that prevented Colorado's defense from loading up against the pass. Salo's ten receptions provided a consistent outlet that extended drives without demanding the explosive plays that Anderson kept chasing.

Colorado's defense recorded eight tackles from Sanders and held firm against the run, but the pass rush never materialized into sustained pressure on Cortez. Clemson's front four corners and safeties allowed the back end to play coverage without help over the top, forcing Anderson into longer developing plays that burned clock. The Buffaloes' scoring came in bunches, but never at moments that shifted momentum—a critical difference when the opponent controls the line of scrimmage on both sides.
aiStory.turningPoint: Clemson's ground game establishing itself early forced Colorado into predictable passing situations, allowing the Tigers' secondary to play confidently while the offense sustained drives that kept the Buffaloes' offense sidelined.

unsungHero.name: Salo
unsungHero.why: Ten catches for 98 yards kept drives alive without demanding highlight-reel plays, allowing Cortez to operate efficiently while Anderson had to force production through the air

story.headline: LSU takes control late, tops Penn State 23-13
body: LSU asserted control when it mattered in a 23-13 win over Penn State in Week 4 PCFL action, with Garrett Nussmeier earning Player of the Game honors after going for 18 of 29 for 199 yards and 2 TDs.
The Tigers outgained Penn State 443–224 in total offense, including 250 yards on the ground. Penn State kept fighting, managing 224 total yards on the night.
Up next, LSU hosts Georgia in Week 5.

aiStory.headline: LSU's ground game overwhelms Penn State in decisive road victory
body: LSU's coaching staff had one clear directive coming into Week 4, and they executed it with ruthless efficiency: establish the run, control the line of scrimmage, and let the passing game operate off play-action. Ferguson carried 10 times for 79 yards, part of a 250-yard rushing attack that made Penn State's defense chase ghosts all afternoon. The Nittany Lions' front seven had no answer for the gap discipline LSU maintained, and that imbalance showed up on the scoreboard in the second half when the Tigers pulled away.

Nussmeier's two touchdown passes came with time to work because LSU's run game had already softened the Penn State secondary. He completed 18 of 29 for 199 yards, but efficiency mattered more than volume here. Chapman's six receptions for 63 yards gave the Tigers a reliable outlet, and the timing of those throws felt measured rather than desperate. Penn State's Myers moved the ball adequately in spots—155 passing yards in a game where his team was perpetually behind—but Jenkins and the ground game mustered only 35 yards on six carries. That's the game right there.

The turnover battle favored Penn State, but it didn't matter. Three LSU fumbles sounds damaging until you realize the Tigers never lost the ball and still dominated field position and time of possession. Penn State's defense, led by Dixon's six tackles, couldn't generate pressure or create the explosive negative plays needed to flip momentum. LSU's methodical approach—grinding yards, converting third downs with the run—slowly suffocated any hope the Nittany Lions had of staying competitive. This wasn't a shootout or a defensive showcase. It was a masterclass in building a lead through toughness and refusing to beat yourself.
aiStory.turningPoint: Ferguson's sustained rushing success in the second half forced Penn State to abandon coverage discipline, giving Nussmeier easy throws on play-action and allowing LSU to build a two-possession lead that Penn State could never threaten.

unsungHero.name: Ferguson
unsungHero.why: His 10 carries anchored LSU's ground-control approach and opened up the passing game, yet Nussmeier took the game ball because quarterbacks always do when they throw touchdowns, even when the back did the heavy lifting.

story.headline: Instant classic: UCLA edges Ohio State, 31-28
body: UCLA found just enough in a 31-28 thriller over Ohio State in Week 4 PCFL action, with Nico Iamaleava earning Player of the Game honors after going for 16 of 34 for 217 yards and 3 TDs.
The Bruins outgained Ohio State 345–338 in total offense, including 132 yards on the ground. Ohio State kept fighting, managing 338 total yards on the night.
Up next, UCLA hosts USC in Week 5.

aiStory.headline: UCLA's precision passing overcomes Ohio State in defensive slugfest
body: Nico Iamaleava threaded the needle when it mattered most, and that surgical accuracy proved the difference as UCLA escaped Columbus with a 31-28 victory over Ohio State in a game decided by efficiency rather than volume. Both offenses moved the ball in fits and starts, but the Bruins' ability to convert on third downs and avoid self-inflicted wounds gave them the edge in a contest where neither team dominated the line of scrimmage.

Ohio State's ground game outgained UCLA by 12 rushing yards, and the Buckeyes' passing attack actually logged 21 more yards through the air. Yet the turnover margin told the real story: UCLA protected the football flawlessly while Ohio State coughed it up once, a fumble that loomed large in a three-point game. Sayin orchestrated enough offense to keep the home crowd engaged, completing 27 passes and directing two scoring drives, but the Buckeyes' offense stalled when it needed sustained rhythm.

The UCLA defense imposed its will in subtle but critical ways. Schwesinger's six tackles and pair of sacks created pressure that forced Ohio State into third-and-long situations the Buckeyes couldn't consistently escape. When Iamaleava needed to deliver—completing 16 of 34 attempts but maximizing what he did throw for—his receivers found soft spots and he fired strikes. Berger's 89 rushing yards on seven carries established enough threat to keep Ohio State honest, preventing the Buckeyes from stacking the box.
aiStory.turningPoint: Ohio State's fumble in the third quarter, which UCLA converted into a field goal three plays later, shifted momentum at a moment when the Buckeyes had clawed back to within one score.

unsungHero.name: Schwesinger
unsungHero.why: While Iamaleava earned POG honors for his passing efficiency, Schwesinger's six tackles and two sacks were the physical anchor that allowed UCLA's secondary to operate with more comfort and forced Ohio State into predictable passing situations.

story.headline: USC takes control late, tops Washington 31-20
body: USC asserted control when it mattered in a 31-20 win over Washington in Week 4 PCFL action, with Woody Marks earning Player of the Game honors after going for 148 rushing yards and 1 rushing TD.
The Trojans outgained Washington 499–321 in total offense, including 242 yards on the ground. Washington kept fighting, managing 321 total yards on the night.
Up next, USC travels to face UCLA in Week 5.

aiStory.headline: USC's Ground Game Overwhelms Washington in Pac-12 Offensive Showcase
body: USC's ground attack proved too much for Washington to contain on Saturday, as the Trojans rushed for 242 yards and leaned heavily on that advantage to build a comfortable 31-20 victory. Woody Marks was the obvious engine, grinding out 148 yards on 11 carries and punching in a touchdown, but the real story was USC's willingness to establish the run early and often while Washington's defense struggled to set the edge.

QB Williams Jr. had a respectable afternoon for Washington, completing 15 of 29 passes for 278 yards with two touchdowns, and wideout James gave the Huskies moments of vertical threat with 92 receiving yards on six catches. But the volume tells the tale: Washington managed only 73 rushing yards on 10 attempts, forcing their offense into obvious passing situations where USC's secondary could tighten coverage. The Trojans' QB Moss didn't need to be spectacular—20 of 31 for 275 yards—because the running game kept chains moving and kept Washington's defense on their heels.

Clean football helped USC's cause as well. Neither team turned the ball over, which meant Washington couldn't engineer the fortunate takeaway their struggling run defense desperately needed. By the time Washington found rhythm passing down the stretch, USC had already built enough cushion to manage the game and control tempo with the running back rotation.
aiStory.turningPoint: USC's sustained scoring drive in the second quarter, capped by a Marks touchdown, shifted momentum decisively as Washington's defensive front failed to maintain gap integrity against the power running scheme.

unsungHero.name: Beard
unsungHero.why: Three receptions for 79 yards might not jump off the stat sheet, but each came on crucial third-down conversions that kept USC's methodical drives alive while the run game punished Washington's front seven.

story.headline: Oklahoma steamrolls Michigan, 45-24
body: Oklahoma dominated from start to finish in a 45-24 rout over Michigan in Week 4 PCFL action, with Sam Franklin earning Player of the Game honors after going for 75 rushing yards and 3 rushing TDs.
The Sooners outgained Michigan 422–387 in total offense, including 204 yards on the ground. Michigan kept fighting, managing 387 total yards on the night.
Up next, Oklahoma travels to face Penn State in Week 5.

aiStory.headline: Oklahoma's ground game overwhelms Michigan in wire-to-wire rout
body: Oklahoma's rushing attack proved too much for Michigan's defense on Saturday, as the Sooners controlled the line of scrimmage from kickoff to final whistle. Sam Franklin's 204 combined rushing yards set the tone early, and Oklahoma never relinquished momentum. While Michigan's passing game found moments of rhythm through Gabriel's 290 yards and Charleston's five receptions, the Wolverines couldn't generate enough explosive plays to keep pace with a Sooners offense that dictated tempo and field position throughout the afternoon.

The decisive schism came in the second quarter when Oklahoma's rushing attack began to overwhelm Michigan's interior. Bernard punched in a touchdown on the ground, and Walsh added a receiving score that extended Oklahoma's lead to a margin the Wolverines could not bridge. Gabriel moved the ball competently in stretches, but Michigan's offense stalled whenever it needed a critical third down, while Oklahoma's ground-first approach methodically drained clock and forced Michigan into reactive mode. A late fumble by Michigan summed up the afternoon: the Wolverines were chasing rather than leading.

Oklahoma's defense, anchored by Jones's nine tackles, bent but rarely broke under Michigan's air assault. The Sooners allowed 283 passing yards yet surrendered only a single touchdown, a testament to scheme and pressure timing. For all of Gabriel's completion percentage and yardage, Michigan's red zone efficiency evaporated when it mattered most. Oklahoma's staff engineered a game plan that exploited Michigan's defensive gaps in run support and executed with the kind of discipline that wins games on the road.
aiStory.turningPoint: Bernard's rushing touchdown in the second quarter expanded Oklahoma's lead and shifted momentum irreversibly, as Michigan's defense lost structural integrity against the ground attack.

unsungHero.name: Jones
unsungHero.why: Nine tackles and constant presence in the backfield made Franklin's rushing lanes possible and kept Michigan's passing game under siege; his work doesn't appear in the stat line but shaped field position all afternoon

story.headline: Notre Dame pulls away from Oregon, 31-21
body: Notre Dame asserted control when it mattered in a 31-21 win over Oregon in Week 4 PCFL action, with Hollywood Smothers earning Player of the Game honors after going for 131 rushing yards and 2 rushing TDs.
The Fighting Irish outgained Oregon 449–375 in total offense, including 205 yards on the ground. Oregon kept fighting, managing 375 total yards on the night.
Up next, Notre Dame hosts Colorado in Week 5.

aiStory.headline: Notre Dame's ground game overwhelms Oregon in defensive grind
body: Notre Dame's offense ground down Oregon's defense with relentless execution on the ground, with running back Hollywood Smothers punishing the Oregon front seven on 21 carries. Smothers finished with 131 yards and two touchdowns, setting the tone early and controlling the line of scrimmage throughout the afternoon. While the scoring margin appears comfortable at ten points, the game was tighter than the final tally suggests—Oregon's pass catchers, particularly receiver Bryant with 108 yards, kept drives alive and forced Notre Dame to stay alert on the edges.

Quarterback Thomas of Oregon played a clean game without turning the ball over, completing 10 of 17 passes for 151 yards and two scores. But his limited volume through the air reflected a defense that dominated possession time. Notre Dame's quarterback Carr managed the tempo effectively, connecting on 19 completions while forcing fewer explosive plays downfield. The turnover battle stayed even on the fumble front, but Notre Dame's offense simply ran out the clock with Smothers serving as the primary engine, wearing down Oregon's pursuit as the game progressed.

The turning point came early in the third quarter when Notre Dame's ground assault shifted into another gear, with Smothers finding creases that Oregon's interior defense could not consistently fill. Oregon had hung close through halftime but lacked the sustained drives to match Notre Dame's ground-control strategy. The coaching room's commitment to running the football in the second half paid dividends, squeezing out a ten-point victory that reflected field position and time of possession more than overall offensive sparkle.
aiStory.turningPoint: Smothers' continued effectiveness running downhill in the third quarter forced Oregon to abandon its defensive scheme, opening passing lanes that Notre Dame exploited to build separation.

unsungHero.name: Thomas
unsungHero.why: His efficiency and ball security kept Oregon competitive despite running the offense out of an uphill position; a ten-for-seventeen day with two touchdowns usually beats you, but his cleanness kept the door open.

weeklyWrap.headline: Texas dominates as LSU, Notre Dame hold the line
weeklyWrap.subhead: Week 4 saw the contenders separate from the pretenders, with Texas sending a message and the middle tier holding firm.
weeklyWrap.lead: The power poll's top five all won this week, but not all victories were created equal. Texas dismantled Boston College with surgical efficiency while the chasing pack—LSU, Notre Dame, and Arkansas—ground out wins that proved depth and resilience matter as much as flash.

segments.title: Texas sends a sledgehammer message
segments.body: Texas did not come to compete; they came to announce. Forty-three points against Boston College reads like a mercy ruling, and it was. The staff showed no mercy in any phase, establishing control early and running the clock as much as the scoreboard. This is what separation looks like in Week 4.

segments.title: UCLA steals Ohio State in a shocker
segments.body: The power poll had no UCLA in sight, yet there they were, escaping Columbus with a one-point win. Ohio State came to play and nearly got it done, but UCLA's poise down the stretch—converting when it mattered most—delivered a statement that depth at the top of the league is thinner than it looks.

segments.title: Arkansas-Tennessee Razorback firepower prevails
segments.body: Arkansas-Tennessee turned into a track meet, and the Razorbacks' offense simply had one more gear. Thirty-eight points indicates a unit that can outscore almost anyone on a given Saturday. Tennessee hung around but ultimately couldn't keep pace, a telling sign for their playoff aspirations if the defense tightens up.

segments.title: Penn State's secondary gets torched by LSU
segments.body: LSU's passing game made Penn State's secondary look pedestrian, a 23-13 margin that understates how thoroughly LSU controlled the air. This defense has gaps, and LSU found them repeatedly. For Penn State, watching tape this week won't be pleasant; for LSU, it's another sign the offense is starting to hit stride.

segments.title: Oklahoma's explosion raises Big questions
segments.body: Oklahoma put 45 on Michigan in a performance that begs explanation. Michigan came in with expectations; Oklahoma came in hungry and stayed that way all night. The Sooners are trending upward at exactly the right time, and after a murky first month, suddenly they look like a team that could make noise in November.

weeklyWrap.closer: Week 5 will tell us whether these performances hold up or if the league's middle tier is due for a correction.
